title: Add read-only observability manifest for autonomy health-review cron
summary: Non-interactive cron health reviews currently have weak visibility into workspace-local observability artifacts because directory listing requires exec approval and read cannot enumerate directories. Add a small read-only manifest/report path so cron jobs can inspect recent anomalies/deltas without elevated or interactive listing.
problem: The autonomy health-review job could not enumerate `.openclaw` artifact paths in a non-interactive run. `exec` required manual approval for directory listing on gateway, while `read` on directories returned EISDIR. This leaves the job able to inspect only already-known file paths, reducing anomaly/delta detection quality and making recurring issue detection weaker than intended.
why_now: This is a durable workflow gap for autonomous monitoring: if artifact discovery is blocked, future health reviews will continue to under-observe the system and miss proposal-worthy recurrences.

changes: Create a stable machine-readable manifest or latest-report file under workspace-local `.openclaw/` that summarizes recent autonomy health signals, anomaly counts, and artifact pointers.
Adjust the health-review cron wording/runbook to consult the manifest first, then read only explicitly listed files.
Keep the manifest read-only from the cron's perspective; generation can happen in the producing workflow or report writer.

constraints: Do not alter auth, approval, routing root, trust boundary, or authority rules.
Prefer additive documentation/reporting over behavioral changes.

evidence.detail: Gateway exec for `pwd && ls -la && ... find .openclaw ...` returned approval-pending in a non-interactive cron context, blocking artifact discovery.
Transcript records both the approval-pending exec result and the EISDIR error when attempting `read` on `.openclaw` as a directory.
Search for prior autonomy-loop anomalies/proposals returned no indexed memory hits, increasing reliance on direct artifact discovery.

expected_benefit: Health-review cron can perform deterministic, non-interactive anomaly scanning.
Recurring issue detection becomes more reliable.
Proposal generation can be evidence-based without needing directory exec approvals.
